nativeRange.habitat: Captive-bred selectively for centuries from wild-type Carassius auratus; commercially produced in China and Japan. Wild ancestor inhabits slow-moving rivers, ponds, lakes, and eutrophic freshwater.

decorPreferences: smooth rounded substrate (sand or rounded gravel)
hardy plants or silk plants (avoid sharp decor that can tear fins)

diet.notes: Best fed sinking goldfish pellets to reduce air-gulping (floating pellets are associated with swim bladder issues in egg-body morphs). Supplement with blanched vegetables (peas, spinach, zucchini) and frozen/live foods such as brine shrimp and daphnia. Feed small amounts twice daily; avoid overfeeding.

avoidWith: fast-swimming single-tail goldfish (Comet, Common) — outcompete for food
fish with impaired vision (Bubble Eye, Telescope/Black Moor) should not share with aggressive feeders

summary: Collective entry for selectively-bred egg-body twin-tail goldfish varieties (Oranda, Ranchu, Ryukin, Fantail, Black Moor, Bubble Eye, Celestial, Pearlscale, Lionhead, Pom Pom). Coldwater; heater generally not required. Intermediate care due to swim bladder susceptibility and water-quality demands.
careNotes:
GENUS-LEVEL ENTRY — covers all twin-tail egg-body fancy goldfish varieties collectively. Individual variety entries: [oranda], [ranchu], [ryukin], [fantail-goldfish], [black-moor]. Cross-reference also [common-goldfish], [comet-goldfish], [shubunkin].

VARIETIES included in this collective entry: Oranda (wen + twin-tail), Ranchu (no dorsal fin + wen + twin-tail), Ryukin (high back hump + twin-tail), Fantail (quadruple caudal fin + egg body), Black Moor / Telescope Eye (protruding eyes + all-black coloration), Lionhead (extreme wen covering head), Bubble Eye (fluid-filled sacs beneath upward-pointing eyes — extremely delicate), Celestial (upward-pointing eyes + no dorsal), Pearlscale (spherical body + domed scales), Pom Pom (narial bouquets). Veiltail is an additional variety with extremely long flowing fins.

SWIM BLADDER DISEASE: The compressed egg-body shape displaces the swim bladder, making these varieties particularly prone to buoyancy disorders. Common triggers include air-gulping from floating foods, constipation, bacterial infections, and rapid temperature changes. Sinking foods strongly preferred. Feeding a blanched, shelled pea after a 24-hour fast is a widely-used first-line treatment.

IMPAIRED-VISION VARIETIES: Bubble Eye and Telescope/Black Moor have severely reduced vision and should be housed with other slow, similarly-impaired fish, or kept separately from fast feeders. Avoid sharp decor that can damage their eye sacs or protruding eyes.

COLDWATER REQUIREMENT: Fancy goldfish are coolwater fish; temperatures consistently above 23–24°C are generally considered stressful for most varieties. A heater is typically not needed in climate-controlled indoor environments. Avoid housing with tropical species.

TANK SIZE: Fancy goldfish are heavy waste producers. 20 US gallons (approx. 76L) per fish is the widely-cited minimum; 150L+ for a pair is generally advised, with more space beneficial for long-term health. Bowls are not suitable.

FILTRATION: Strong biological filtration is important due to their high ammonia output. Hang-on-back or canister filters with low-flow outlets are recommended to avoid buffeting the fish.

LONGEVITY: Well-maintained fancy goldfish have been reported to live 10–20 years. Most die prematurely in inadequate conditions (bowls, tropical tanks, overfeeding).
breedingNotes: Egg scatterers that tend to spawn in spring or when water temperature rises. Aquarium spawning can be triggered by gradually raising temperature toward the upper end of their range. Eggs are adhesive and attach to plants or surfaces; parents will consume eggs if not separated promptly. Fry require infusoria or commercial fry foods initially, graduating to finely crushed flake. Sexing is easier during spawning season when males develop breeding tubercles on their gill plates.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Primary goldfish care guide; covered tank sizing (20 gal/fish), temperature (50–70°F / 10–21°C), diet, and general husbandry. Dedicated fancy-goldfish URL returned 404; general care guide URL used.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (4–8 in / 10–20 cm), temperature (50–75°F / 10–24°C), pH (6.5–7.5), tank size (20 gal/fish), lifespan (5–10 yrs, up to 20 for Veiltail), temperament, compatible tankmates, varieties, and swim bladder disease info. Minor disagreement on temperature max (21°C vs 24°C) — used 23°C as average, consistent with user-supplied brief. Lifespan upper end aligned with user brief (10–20 yrs for well-kept specimens).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy (Cyprinidae / Cypriniformes), native range (East Asia, China, Japan), wild habitat description, wild adult size max 48 cm (not applicable to egg-body fancy varieties — used hobby sources for captive size), wild depth range 0–20 m. FishBase wild temperature (0–41°C) not used for aquarium parameters.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterers; parents consume eggs. Trigger spawning by gradually warming water. Raise fry separately.
